segments.s569-g17: شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day' placed before the verb. یکسر همی are adverbial intensifiers: یکسر 'entirely/continually' and the archaic particle همی marks repeated/continuous action in narrative past. تاختند is the past plural of تاختن ('to rush/gallop') and is the main verb: 'they rode/galloped continuously.'
segments.s569-g18: به خواب و به خوردن are adverbial objects/activities introduced by the preposition به ('towards/into' here marking the activities 'sleep' and 'eating'). نپرداختند is the negative past plural of پرداختن with the negative prefix ن-; here it means 'they did not attend to / did not take part in' (i.e. 'they did not sleep or eat').
segments.s569-g19: برین‌گونه is an adverb 'thus/in this way' that frames the action. از شهر marks origin ('from the city') with از + noun. بر before خورستان is a directional preposition ('toward/to Khuristan'). Word order places these prepositional phrases before the verb.
segments.s569-g20: همی + راند expresses continued action in the past ('they kept driving/riding'); راند is the past form 'drove/rode'. تا here means 'as far as' (marking extent/destination) and governs کشور سورستان 'the country/realm of Suristan'.
segments.s569-g21: چو introduces a temporal clause here ('when'). اسب و تن is the compound subject 'the horse and (the) body/person' (i.e. steed and rider). از + تاختن uses the verbal noun تاختن 'galloping/charging' with از to mark cause ('from galloping'). گشت is the past of گشتن in the sense 'became/turned' and links to the predicate adjective سست 'weak'.
segments.s569-g22: فرود آمدن is a verbal-noun phrase 'to alight/dismount' (فرود = descent/alighting + آمدن = to come). را is the definite object marker attaching to the following object. همی جست uses the narrative particle همی with جست (here 'sought/searched') to mean 'they looked for'; جای ('place') is the object of جست, so the whole phrase means 'they sought a place to dismount.'
segments.s569-g23: دهی خرم is a noun phrase (دهی = 'a village/hamlet' + خرم = 'joyful/lush') serving as subject. آمد is past 'came' and the sequence به پیشش indicates 'to his front/before him' (به + پیشش 'in his presence/in front of him'); the second به راه places the event 'on the road' ('onto the road' / 'on the way').
segments.s569-g24: پر از ... is an adjectival phrase 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of'). The list 'باغ و میدان' ('gardens and fields') follows that construction; the last coordination repeats پر before جشنگاه ('full [of] festive places') with the second از omitted by ellipsis, a common poetic economy.
segments.s569-g9: This clause carries the main action: an object and its verb. دو اسپ گرانمایه = “two prized steeds” (اسپ + adjective گرانمایه). ز آخر is a prepositional phrase (ز = “from/of”, آخر = “the rear/end”) indicating source/location. ببرد is an archaic/poetic past (the preverb ب‑ + بردن) here meaning “took/carried off” (third person singular) and links back to the subject phrase that follows in the next segment.
segments.s569-g10: This is the subject/agent phrase that follows the verb (typical in poetic word order). گزیده = “chosen/selected” modifies the following nouns; سلیح here functions adjectivally as “armed” (literally ‘weaponed’); سواران = “horsemen/riders” is the head noun. گرد appears as a short poetic particle/epithet attached to the group (here functioning adverbially/epithetically, hence translated as “a band/of the group”).
segments.s569-g11: ز دینار = “of/from dinars” (here listing money as part of the spoils). چندانک is a quantifying/limiting word meaning “to that extent / a certain amount,” and بایست is the modal “ought/should be” (present/modal obligation). نیز = “also/likewise,” linking this clause to the following list of other valuables.
segments.s569-g12: This is a continuation of the list introduced by نیز. ز marks each item (“of/with”); خوشاب here means a pleasant alcoholic drink or ‘good wine’ (a luxury item); و = “and”; یاقوت = “ruby” (gem); و هرگونه چیز = “and every kind of thing” (هرگونه = “any kind / every sort”, چیز = “thing”). The whole segment lists other valuables.
segments.s569-g13: چو آمد = “when came/when it came” (چو = “when”, آمد = past of ‘to come/fall’), followed by همه ساز رفتن به جای = “all the preparations for going/for departure.” Here ساز is used in the sense of means/preparations (device), رفتن = the verbal noun ‘going/departure’, and به جای = “in place/for departure,” so the whole phrase means the preparations to depart were complete.
segments.s569-g14: This gives the result: شب آمد = “night came/night fell.” دو تن = “two persons” (تن = person); راست کردند رای is an idiomatic phrase: راست (literally “straight/true”) with کردند + رای (“opinion/plan”) = “they made/firmly fixed a plan / they resolved.” The verb کردند is simple past plural, so the two people decided.
segments.s569-g15: سوی شهر ایران = “toward the city of Iran” (سوی = toward, شهر = city, ایران = Iran). نهادند is the past tense plural of نهادن (to set/put), here ‘they set/turned’; روی = “face” (literally ‘they set their face toward’), a common Persian idiom meaning ‘they headed for / they set out for.’
segments.s569-g16: This is a compact adjectival description of the two travelers. دو = “two”; خرم = “joyful/happy”; نهان here functions adverbially as “hidden/secretly” (they keep themselves concealed); شاد = “glad/happy”; و = “and”; آرامجوی = a compound adjective ‘seeker of rest/peace’ (someone who seeks calm). These adjectives all modify the pair mentioned earlier.
segments.s569-g73: This is an optative (wish) construction: the preposition به introduces the beneficiary/target phrase 'بدخواه ما' ('our ill-wisher'), followed by the optative particle باد ('may it be') and then the measure 'چندان زیان' ('so much harm'). Word order is: به + NP (beneficiary) + باد (optative) + degree + noun. 'بدخواه ما' is literally 'one who wishes ill of us' = 'our enemy'.
segments.s569-g74: The conjunction/relative که introduces the clause that specifies the comparison/measure: 'that (which) from Caesar came to the Iranians.' از ... آمد ... به is a straightforward agent→action→recipient sequence: 'came from Caesar to the Iranians' (i.e. 'that Caesar brought upon the Iranians'). آمد is simple past 'came/brought'.
segments.s569-g75: از ایران is the locative/source phrase 'from Iran' placed before the verb. پراگنده شد is a verbal phrase 'was scattered / became scattered' (پراگنده = scattered, شد = became). هرک بود is an inverted relative/indefinite clause 'whoever he was' (هرک = هر که). The sequence thus expresses that everyone was driven/scattered out of Iran.
segments.s569-g76: نماند is negative existential/past 'did not remain / there remained not.' اندران بوم is a locative phrase 'in that/among them the homeland' (اندران = اندر + ان = 'in/among that'), specifying where nothing remained. کشت و درود names what did not remain; کشت here is 'cultivation' (agriculture). درود is difficult/archaic in this context (probably a word related to harvest or condition), so the semantic label is uncertain.
segments.s569-g77: ز بس is a causal/degree phrase 'from/for so much' (i.e. 'because of so much'). It introduces the reasons: غارت و کشتن ('plunder and killing') of مرد و زن ('men and women'). Note کشتن is the verbal noun 'killing' here, forming a coordinated nominal phrase.
segments.s569-g78: پراگنده گشت is verb + auxiliary meaning 'became scattered / was dispersed' (پراگنده = scattered, گشت = became). The subject follows: آن بزرگ انجمن 'that great assembly' — typical poetic word order putting the predicate before the noun phrase it applies to.
segments.s569-g79: وزیشان is a contracted combination (و ز ایشان = 'and of them / and from them'), introducing a subset of people. بسی نیز is an intensifying adverbial 'very many / also,' and ترسا شدند is a predicate meaning 'became TERSA (pious/ascetic)' — ترسا is 'a pious person / ascetic' and شدند is past plural 'they became.'
segments.s569-g80: به زنار is a prepositional/means phrase 'with a girdle' or 'putting on a girdle' (زنار = girdle). پیش سکوبا شدند literally 'they came/stood before سكوبا (skuba)'; پیش marks position 'in front of/before' and شدند is 'they became/were.' سکوبا is an obscure/rare term here (context suggests a court office such as 'cupbearer' or an attendant), so the sense is uncertain but the structure is preposition + noun + verb.
segments.s569-g65: همی here is a poetic continuous/iterative marker ('ever'/'as if'); بود is the past/copular auxiliary producing a past/hypothetical frame ('it was (that) ...'). تاج آید is the predication where تاج (crown) is the subject and آید (literally 'comes') expresses appearance or coming-into-view; از موی تو is a prepositional phrase ('from/out of your hair') specifying the source. Word order is literary: the auxiliary+particle (همی بود) sets the scene, then the noun + verb + source phrase give the image.
segments.s569-g66: Parallel structure to the previous segment: همی again marks the poetic continuous/reenactment sense; رنگ عاج is a compound where رنگ ('color/complexion') is qualified by عاج ('ivory'), so together 'the ivory hue/complexion'. آید ('comes') expresses that this hue seems to spring forth; از روی تو ('from your face') gives the source. The whole phrase pictures an appearance emanating from the addressee's face.
segments.s569-g67: بخندید is a past/simple perfect verb 'laughed' placed before its subject (poetic V–S order): شاپور is the subject. و ties the clauses; بستد here means 'ceased/put an end to' (figuratively 'stopped/laid aside') and governs نبید ('wine'), so بستد نبید = 'he stopped/put aside the wine.'
segments.s569-g68: یکی functions as an indefinite/article-like introducer ('a' / 'one') to present a sudden occurrence; باد سرد ('a cold wind') is the noun phrase, از جگر ('from the liver') uses جگر metaphorically for the heart/seat of feeling, and برکشید is the past verb 'drew up/rose'—so the clause means 'a cold wind rose from his heart.' Word order is poetic but straightforward: introducer + noun phrase + source + verb.
segments.s569-g69: به + پالیزبان is the indirect object ('to the gardener'); گفت is the verb 'said' (here used to introduce direct speech); کای is the vocative 'O' (addressing someone) and پاک‌دین is the addressee's epithet/name. The sequence literally: 'to the gardener he said, "O Pak-din..."'.
segments.s569-g70: چه آگاهی is the question phrase 'what knowledge'; استت is the classical copula + enclitic -ت ('is' + 'to you') functioning idiomatically as 'do you have' / 'is there for you', so the construction asks about the addressee's possession/awareness. ز ایران‌زمین ('of/from the land of Iran') specifies the domain of that knowledge.
segments.s569-g71: چنین دادپاسخ is a compound noun/verb phrase meaning 'thus he answered' or 'he gave such a reply.' که‌ای here functions to introduce the reported speech with a vocative flavor (که + ای), and برمنش is the addressee adjective 'noble‑minded' (vocative 'O noble-minded one').
segments.s569-g72: ز تو دور بادا is an optative clause: ز تو دور ('far from you') + بادا ('may it be') = 'may (he/it) be far from you.' بد modifies بدکنش (an intensifying adjective before the noun), and بدکنش is a noun 'ill‑doer'/'one who does evil'—so the whole clause prays that the evil doer be kept far from the addressee. Word order is emphatic/poetic, with the optative particle following the predicate.
segments.s569-g81: This short nominal phrase lists a quantifier + title and a prepositional phrase describing appearance. 'بس' is an archaic/poetic quantifier (here effectively "many" or "many a") placed before the noun. 'جاثلیقی' (Catholicos) is the head noun. The following prepositional cluster 'به سر بر کلاه' is a frozen word order (prep + noun + postposed noun) meaning "with a hat on the head"; literally 'to head on hat' in older Persian word order.
segments.s569-g82: 'به دور از' is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning "far from" (literally "at a distance from"). Word order is particle/preposition + adjective + 'از' + coordinated nouns. 'بر و بوم' is a conventional pairing meaning "land/home/homeland"; 'آرامگاه' follows as another object of 'از' giving "from home and from the tomb."
segments.s569-g83: Standard indirect-object fronting: 'بدو' (= "to him") appears before the verb. The verb 'گفت' (here used as "said") follows, and the speaker's name/title 'شاپور شاه' comes after the verb — a common poetic inversion. 'اورمزد' appended names the deity (Ahura Mazda) and functions here as an epithet or invocation attached to the king's name.
segments.s569-g84: The conjunction 'که' introduces reported speech ("that / that which"). 'رخشان' is an adjective "radiant/bright" qualifying 'رخ' (face). 'بدی' in this older poetic context is a verb form (2nd-person past) meaning "you made/gave" (archaic past of دادن); it links the subject (you) to the predicate. 'همچو ماه اورمزد' is a simile 'like the moon of Ohrmazd' modifying how the face became radiant.
segments.s569-g85: 'کجا شد که' is a rhetorical formula meaning "how (did it happen) that..."; the first 'شد' carries the sense 'happen'. The clause 'قیصر چنین چیره شد' is the main assertion: 'قیصر' (Caesar) is the subject, 'چنین' ('thus/so') intensifies, 'چیره' ('dominant/overmastering') is the predicative adjective, and the final 'شد' is the ordinary past copula/verb 'became'.
segments.s569-g86: The phrase begins with the older poetic preposition 'ز' (= 'by/through' or 'from'), introducing cause: 'ز بخت' = 'by fate'. 'آب' here is used figuratively as "water/lifeblood/prosperity" of a people. 'ایرانیان' is genitive (of the Iranians), 'تیره شد' is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'became dark/bleak' (tireh = dark + شد = became), stating the consequence.
segments.s569-g87: Again 'بدو' = 'to him' introduces the indirect object. 'باغبان' (the gardener) is the speaker; 'گفت' is the verb "said." 'کای' is the vocative particle ("O") addressing someone directly; 'سرفراز' is a vocative epithet meaning "exalted/proud/victorious." The order (indirect object — speaker — verb — vocative) is typical of verse inversion.
segments.s569-g88: Here the second-person pronoun + object marker are separated in the text: 'ت' (for 'تو') + 'را' together function as 'ترا' = 'to you'. The phrase 'جاودان مهتری باد' is an optative wish: 'جاودان' (eternal) modifies 'مهتری' (preeminence/chiefship), and 'باد' is the optative particle meaning 'may it be'. The conjunction 'و' adds a second wish 'ناز' (grace/favor/honorable regard) as another thing wished upon the addressee.
segments.s569-g97: This clause opens with the subordinating/relative particle که (here 'that'), followed by دانا as a descriptive noun 'the wise [one]'. زد is the poetical use of زدن meaning 'to perform/utter/recite' (not a physical strike), taking این داستان 'this story' as its object. از نخست is a temporal phrase 'from the first / from the start' that situates the action.
segments.s569-g98: This is a subordinate relative clause introduced by که and headed by هرکس 'whoever'. The second که is the internal relativizer 'who/that'. آزرم is used as a proper name (Azram) and مهمان is the noun 'guest'. نجست is the past form of جستن used here in the sense 'sought' (so the clause says 'whoever did not seek Azram as a guest').
segments.s569-g99: نباشد is a negative existential/copular form here ('is not' / 'will not be') followed by خرد 'wisdom'. هیچ modifies خرد to mean 'no wisdom whatsoever' and نزدیک اوی places that lack near him ('near him' with the older pronoun اوی). The clause states that wisdom will not be close to him.
segments.s569-g100: نیاز آورد is a light-verb construction 'bring need' (آورد, 'brings, causes'), with بخت تاریک 'dark fate/ill luck' as the subject that causes that need; اوی is the enclitic pronoun 'his/him'. The sequence means 'his dark fortune will bring need upon him.'
segments.s569-g101: This is a series of imperative/imperatival commands: باش (here in the form باش/بباش) 'be (at ease)', بیاسای 'rest' and می‌خور 'eat' (poetic/colloquial present/impositive form used like an imperative) followed by به کام 'to/for your desire' meaning 'to your fill'. The verbs are coordinated with و 'and'.
segments.s569-g102: چو here means 'when', introducing a temporal clause. گردد (گردد/گشت) is a subjunctive/future-like 'become', followed by دلت 'your heart' (دل + second-person suffix ت). رام is predicate 'tamed/subdued'. بر گوی نام is an imperative instruction: گوی (here the verbal sense 'say') + نام 'name', with the preposition بر marking the object or direction ('upon/forward'); overall: 'when your heart becomes tame, speak (tell) your name.'
segments.s569-g103: بدو 'to him' marks the indirect object ('said to him'). گفت is the verb 'said' (here used as past narrative) followed by شاپور the speaker's name. کری is an adjective 'deaf' referring to someone just mentioned; رواست is the copular phrase 'is fitting/allowed' (روا + است in contracted poetic form). The whole segment records Shapur's speech: 'It is fitting for the deaf [man].'
segments.s569-g104: به مابر 'to/for us' (مابر = ما + بر, 'on our part' / 'for us') sets the perspective; کنون 'now' is temporal; میزبان 'host' is the predicate noun and پادشاست is a nominal/predicative form meaning 'is a king' or 'is kingly/royal' (poetic copula). Together: 'Now the king is our host' or 'the king is now our host.'
segments.s569-g33: "بدو گفت شاپور کای نیک‌خواه" is arranged in a typically flexible poetic order: بدو (to him) + گفت (said) + شاپور (Shapur) — literally 'to him said Shapur'. کای نیک‌خواه is a vocative address: کای is an archaic/poetic 'O' and نیک‌خواه is an epithet meaning 'kind/benevolent one' addressing the addressee.
segments.s569-g34: سخن چند پرسی is literal word-order 'words few you-ask' = 'you ask a few questions/words'; پرسی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/indicative 'you ask' in classical Persian. ز گم‌کرده راه is a prepositional phrase introduced by poetic ز (= به/از) meaning 'about/from' + گم‌کرده 'lost' modifying راه 'road/path'.
segments.s569-g35: یک مرد ایرانیم راه‌جوی links a simple nominal self-introduction: یک مرد ('one man') + ایرانیم is the predicative 'I am Iranian' attached to the speaker, and راه‌جوی is an appositive noun 'road-seeker' or 'traveler' describing him. The suffixal copula is expressed in ایرانیم rather than a separate verb.
segments.s569-g36: گریزان بدین مرز بنهاده روی is a participial and locative clause: گریزان 'fleeing' modifies the speaker; بدین = به + این ('to/ toward this') introduces مرز 'border'; بنهاده روی is a poetic verbal phrase from نهادن ('to place') + روی 'face' meaning 'has set (his) face toward' (i.e. 'turned his face toward'). Word order is compact and inverted as in classical verse.
segments.s569-g37: پر از دردم از قیصر و لشکرش strings causes and possessions: پر 'full (of)' + از دردم 'of my pain' gives the state, then a second از introduces the cause 'از قیصر و لشکرش' = 'because of Caesar and his army'; لشکرش carries the possessive -ش ('his').
segments.s569-g38: مبادا که بینم ... is an optative/negative wish: مبادا 'may it not be / lest' + که introduces the subordinate clause; بینم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive 'I see' used after مبادا; سر و افسرش is a coordinated direct object ('his head and his helmet/armament'), with the possessive -ش attached to افسر but understood for both nouns.
segments.s569-g39: گر امشب مرا میزبانی کنی is a protasis ('if' clause): گر = 'if'; امشب 'tonight' sets time; مرا (here segmented as م + را) is the object 'me' with the object marker را; میزبانی 'hosting/lodging' is the noun/verb idea 'host me'; کنی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive 'you do' used in classical Persian to form the conditional apodosis.
segments.s569-g40: هشیواری و مرزبانی کنی continues the apodosis: two coordinated duties 'alertness' and 'border-guarding' (هشیواری و مرزبانی) governed by the same verb کنی ('you keep/do'), so the verb applies to both coordinated nouns.
segments.s569-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The sequence بر زد سر is a verbal phrase in which زد (past) combines with the adverbial/prefix بر to mean 'raised' or 'lifted' (literally 'struck upward') and سر is the noun 'head'; از + برج marks origin 'from the برج'. برج شیر آفتاب is a genitival/noun phrase 'the tower/house of the Lion (i.e. Leo) of the sun' with آفتاب (sun) qualifying the preceding nouns.
segments.s569-g2: ببالید is a past-form verb meaning 'flourished/exulted/brightened' with روز as its subject ('the day'); و links the two coordinated verbs; بپالود is another past-form verb with the prefix بـ again, here used causatively/actively 'drove away/cleared' and خواب is its object 'sleep' (i.e. 'banished sleep').
segments.s569-g3: به جشن آمدند is a verb phrase 'came to the feast' (به + noun = 'to/into'); آنک بودی به شهر is a relative/nominal phrase 'those who were in the town' (آنک = 'who/that', بودی = past of 'to be', به شهر = 'in/to the city'). Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis: relative phrase (آنک بودی به شهر) qualifies the people who انجام آمدند.
segments.s569-g4: This short NP lists who came: بزرگان جوینده is a compound noun phrase 'the great (بزرگان) [and] the seekers (جوینده)' — جوینده is a participial/noun form 'seeker'. از جشن بهر is a prepositional phrase 'for the sake of the feast' or 'from the celebration toward [it]' where از marks origin/association and بهر indicates purpose/for the sake of.
segments.s569-g5: کنیزک (diminutive of کنیز) is the subject 'the maidservant/young slave-girl'; سوی + NP marks direction 'toward (the) چاره' (a remedy/solution); بنهاد is a past verb 'placed/set/turned' and روی ('face') is its object/complement — literally 'she set/turned her face toward a solution.'
segments.s569-g6: چنانچون بود introduces a simile/'just as' clause; مردم is the subject 'the people' and چاره‌جوی is a nominal/participle 'seeking a remedy' that predicates the people's disposition. The construction says the maidservant's action paralleled the general attitude of the people.
segments.s569-g7: چو begins a temporal clause 'when'. ایوان خالی is the noun phrase 'the empty portico/hall'. به + چنگ + آمدش is a light-verb construction: آمدش ('came to him/it', with enclitic -ش) with the prepositional phrase به چنگ meaning roughly 'into (his) grasp' or 'into being seized'; syntactically the whole clause sets the time/background for what follows.
segments.s569-g8: This is a coordinated nominal list — دل شیر و چنگ و پلنگ — followed by آمدش ('came to him'). Grammatically, the nouns function as the things that 'came to him' or 'seized him' (i.e. he was filled/possessed by them); the enclitic -ش on آمد marks the experiencer. The three nouns enumerate qualities or images (heart/courage, the harp (music), and the leopard).
segments.s569-g89: This nominal phrase means “no trace/sign of him, dead or alive.” ازو (az-u) is the older genitive/oblique form ‘of him/of her’ placed before the noun phrase. مرده و زنده is a fixed pairing ‘dead and alive’ (here functioning like modern ‘dead or alive’), and جایی نشان is a noun phrase ‘any sign/trace’ with نشان ‘trace’ as the head.
segments.s569-g90: نیامد is a past negative verb ‘did not come/appear.’ به ایران is the locative/directional phrase ‘to/in Iran.’ The final three pieces (ب + دان + سرکشان) are an older/poetic sequence: ب likely marks direction ‘to/into,’ while دان and سرکشان here are part of a phrase meaning ‘for/unto those proud/rebellious ones’ — the exact morphological pieces are archaic/poetic and the supplied glosses do not fit perfectly, so the core sense is that no trace reached those chiefs in Iran.
segments.s569-g91: هرانکس که is a relative quantifier ‘whoever / all those who.’ بودند is the past plural of بودن ‘they were’ and here sets the background status ‘who were from…’. ز آبادبوم uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) ‘from’ plus آبادبوم ‘the settled/native/inhabited land.’
segments.s569-g92: اسیرند is a present/state verb ‘they are captives.’ سرتاسر is an adverb ‘throughout/entirely’ modifying the clause; اکنون ‘now’ locates the time; به روم gives the destination/place ‘in/at Rome (Byzantium).’ Word order places the predicate at the start (typical in poetry) and finishes with the place.
segments.s569-g93: برین is the fused/poetic combination of بر + این ‘upon this/at this,’ placing the reaction to the preceding matter. زار qualifies the weeping — here in the sense of lamentation/bitterly — and بگریست is simple past ‘he wept.’ پالیزبان (the subject placed after the verb, as is common in poetry) is ‘the gardener/groundsman.’
segments.s569-g94: که introduces a relative clause ‘who.’ بود is past ‘was,’ آن زمان ‘that time’ gives the temporal phrase, and شاه را میزبان is the object/topic construction meaning ‘the king’s host/host of the king’ (را marks شاه as definite/direct object or topic). The clause identifies the gardener by his role at that time.
segments.s569-g95: بدو is the dative/enclitic ‘to him.’ میزان here functions as a speaking person’s name or title (in context a steward/official), and گفت introduces direct speech ‘said.’ کایدر is an archaic/poetic interrogative/adverbial (here best read as ‘for/while’ or introducing the time-span) followed by سه روز ‘three days’ specifying the duration.
segments.s569-g96: بباشی is the jussive/imperative form ‘you stay/remain’ addressed to the addressee. بود here is a past copula used in a verbless predicative sense (poetic usage), and خانه گیتی فروز is an emphatic noun phrase ‘the house that brightens/illuminates the world’ (گیتی فروز = world-illuminating, an epithet).
segments.s569-g41: برآنم is a fixed classical phrase (بر + آن + ـم) meaning 'I am of that mind / I intend' in the present stative; it introduces a subordinate clause with که as a complementizer. که here is the complementizer 'that'. روزی is an adverb 'one day / someday' (literally 'when a day dawns') modifying the verb phrase. به کار آیدت is literal 'comes to use/works for you' (به = 'to/for', کار = 'use/work', آیدت = 'it comes to you' with enclitic -ت 'to you'), so the whole segment means 'I intend that someday it will come to your use / be useful to you.'
segments.s569-g42: درختی is the indefinite noun 'a tree' (نکره with -ی). که is the relative/complementizer 'that/which' introducing the result clause. کشتی is 'ship / vessel' and به بار آیدت literally 'will come to load for you' or more idiomatically 'will yield a load for your ship' (به = 'to/for', بار = 'load/cargo', آیدت = 'it comes to you / befalls you' used here of producing cargo).
segments.s569-g43: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او → بدو) marking the indirect object. باغبان is the speaker 'the gardener.' گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical past). کین is used here as a demonstrative 'this/that' (classical usage), and خان means 'khan' in the sense 'inn / caravanserai' or 'house' — together 'کین خان تست' is reported speech 'this khan is yours' (تست is the copula/possession marker 'is/it is yours' in older Persian).
segments.s569-g44: تن باغبان literally 'the body/person of the gardener' = 'the gardener himself.' نیز is 'also / likewise.' مهمان is 'guest.' تست again is the older/copular form meaning 'is yours' or 'is (for) you.' So the clause states 'the gardener himself is also your guest.'
segments.s569-g45: بدان is the prepositional phrase به + آن 'with regard to that / concerning that' (written here split). چیز 'thing' names the matter. کاید is a noun meaning 'plot / cunning plan.' مرا (here split as م + را) is the first-person object clitic 'to/for me' plus the object marker را; together دست‌رس 'at hand / within reach' forms the phrase 'دست‌رسِ مرا' (elliptically) meaning 'within my reach' or 'accessible to me.' The clause therefore means 'concerning that plot, (so that) it be within my reach.'
segments.s569-g46: بکوشم is the present/subjunctive first-person singular of کوشیدن with the prefix بـ (classical present/future sense): 'I will/shall try.' بیارم is the verb 'bring (it) in/bring it about' with first-person ending (-م) — here 'I will bring/get it.' نگویم is the negative present/subjunctive/indicative 'I will not say / I do not tell.' به کس 'to anyone' (به = 'to/for', کس = 'any person/anyone'). Together: 'I will try to get it within my reach, I will bring it and tell no one.'
segments.s569-g47: فرود آمد is a verbal phrase 'he alighted / he descended' (فرود = descent/alighting, آمد = came). از is the preposition 'from.' باره here means 'occasion / on the matter of / from the retinue/charge' (classical usages vary; context shows movement away from Shapur's entourage or occasion). شاپور شاه is the name + title 'Shapur the king.' The segment states 'he descended from Shapur Shah's (retinue/occasion).'
segments.s569-g48: کنیزک is the diminutive 'young maidservant / little slave-girl' (subject). همی is a classical particle giving the imperfective/continuous sense to the verb (here 'was / kept on'), so همی رفت = 'went on / went along.' با او 'with him.' به راه 'on the road / along the way.' So: 'the little maid went along with him on the road.'
segments.s569-g57: کسی کو می‌آرد نخست او خورد is an older-phrased conditional/relative: کسی کو (کسی که) = “whoever/one who”; می‌آرد is the present/habitual verb “brings”; نخست (adverb) “first” precedes the clause particle او خورد where خورد is a past-form (or simple perfect) “ate/consumed.” Word order is emphatic: the relative phrase (کسی کو می‌آرد) is followed by the result (نخست او خورد).
segments.s569-g58: چو بیشش بود سالیان و خرد gives the reason/circumstance: چو here functions as a temporal/causal conjunction (“when/since”); بیشش = ‘more of him’ (i.e. greater/older) and بود is the past copula “was”; سالیان ‘years’ and خرد ‘wisdom’ are coordinated by و. So the clause says he was greater in years and in wisdom, supplying the rationale for the previous statement.
segments.s569-g59: تو از من به سال اندکی برتری is a comparative statement: تو از من = “you compared with me”; به سال qualifies the comparison in terms of years/age; اندکی = “a little”; برتری = “superiority/advantage.” The phrase literally: “You have a slight superiority over me in years.”
segments.s569-g60: تو باید که چون می‌دهی می‌خوری is a deontic clause with a temporal subordinate: تو باید = “you should/must”; که introduces the following content; چون here means “when/as” (temporal) and governs the subordinate clause می‌دهی (you give); the main verb in the subordinate is می‌خوری (you eat). The sense: “You ought that when you give (it), you eat (it).”
segments.s569-g61: بدو باغبان گفت کای پرهنر: بدو = to him (prepositional/indirect object), باغبان = “the gardener,” گفت here functions as the verb “said” (the given senses list was nominal but the word is used verbally), and کای پرهنر is a vocative: کای = O, پرهنر = “skilled/cultured.” The clause is ‘the gardener said to him: “O cultured one…”’.
segments.s569-g62: نخست آن خورد می‌که با زیب‌تر gives the content of the gardener’s admonition. نخست = “first”; آن = “that/the one”; خورد (here) = “eats” (simple/past form used in narrative/imperative sense); the phrase is inverted: it means roughly “let the one that is more beautiful eat first.” Note that می‌که in the word list looks like an independent lexeme for wine but here the sequence functions as a relative/particle in older word order; با زیب‌تر = “who is more beautiful/with more beauty.”
segments.s569-g63: تو باید که باشی برین پیش رو is a prescriptive clause: تو باید که باشی = “you should be” (باشی is subjunctive/optative ‘be’); برین پیش رو is a compound phrase meaning “foremost/in the first place” (برین is an elevated/archaic superlative-form element + پیش‌رو ‘front/foremost’). The clause instructs the addressee to take precedence.
segments.s569-g64: که پیری به فرهنگ و بر سال نو gives the justification: که = “for/since”; پیری = “seniority/old age”; به فرهنگ = “in culture/manners/refinement”; و بر سال نو = “and (in) years” (here بر + سال + نو indicate superiority with respect to years/age). Together: “since you are senior in breeding and in years.”
segments.s569-g25: A compact descriptive noun phrase. تن (the body / person) is the head; از رنج خسته is a causal postpositional phrase using از + noun + adjective: “tired from suffering.” گریزان ز بد is a participial adjective phrase (گریزان = ‘fleeing/avoiding’) followed by the poetic preposition ز + بد ‘from bad/evil’ — both modify تن. Word order is typical for Persian poetry: head noun + stacked modifiers.
segments.s569-g26: Simple past narrative clause. بیامد is the past tense ‘he came.’ در here is the noun ‘door/gate’ (poetic use) rather than the locative preposition, and باغبانی stands alongside it as a possessive/relational noun (‘of the gardener / pertaining to the garden’) — together در باغبانی = ‘the gardener’s door / the garden gate.’ بزد is the past verb ‘he struck / knocked.’ The sequence is: came — (to) the door — knocked.
segments.s569-g27: Declarative clause: بیامد is the past ‘he came.’ دمان is an adverbial/locative noun meaning ‘edge / near / hem’ (here ‘came near / approached’). مرد پالیزبان is the noun phrase identifying the one who came: مرد = ‘man’ and پالیزبان = ‘gardener/caretaker.’ The order places the verb before the identifying noun phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s569-g28: A relative clause introduced by که ‘who/that.’ هم … هم is the correlative pair ‘both … and …’ modifying the head (the gardener); نیک‌دل and میزبان are predicate nouns/adjectives linked by the past copula بود ‘was,’ which applies to both predicates: ‘who was both kind‑hearted and (also) a host.’
segments.s569-g29: A nominal description of the actors. دو تن is ‘two persons’ (subject). دیده is here a past-participial/epithet ‘seen’ (i.e., ‘two men who were seen’), followed by an instrumental/adverbial list introduced by با: با نیزه و درع و خود = ‘with spear and armor and themselves’ (enumerating their equipment/appearance). The phrase groups descriptive material before the speech-act clause.
segments.s569-g30: Report of speech: ز شاپور پرسید = ‘they asked of Shapur’ (ز used as ‘from / of’). The following clause is an embedded interrogative with inverted, archaic/poetic word order: هست این درود = ‘is this a greeting?’ where هست (copula/existential) precedes the demonstrative phrase این درود. The overall construction is ‘they asked Shapur: “Is this a greeting?”’
segments.s569-g31: Temporal and interrogative introductory phrase. بدین بیگهی is a temporal adverbial meaning ‘on that occasion / at this very time’ (a poetic compound); از کجا is the interrogative prepositional phrase ‘from where’; خاستی is second‑person past ‘you rose / you came’ (you came from where?). The verb appears at the end as typical in Persian; the fronted temporal phrase sets the context for the question.
segments.s569-g32: Follow-up interrogative clause. چنین is an adverb ‘thus / in this manner’ modifying the nominalized action تاختن (the act of charging/attacking). را is the definite/object marker attached to the verbal noun. بیاراستی here is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning roughly ‘did you prepare / did you set yourself to’ (i.e., ‘did you intend/prepare to charge in this way?’). The sequence asks whether the addressee came from where and had intended this kind of charge.
segments.s569-g49: خورش ساخت is a past action: ساخت is the simple past 'made' (3rd-person). The subject is the following noun phrase زن باغبان ('the gardener's wife'); Persian often omits an explicit subject pronoun. چندان here qualifies amount ('so much/many') and links forward to the next clause about extent.
segments.s569-g50: ز هر گونه = 'of/from every kind' (ز is the poetic/genitive 'of/from', هر گونه 'every kind'). چندانک = 'to that extent' (ties to the previous چندان). بودش توان is literal 'there was for her power' — بود + enclitic ش (= 'for her/it was to her') and توان 'ability/power', i.e. 'as much as she could'.
segments.s569-g51: چو here is temporal 'when'. نان خورده شد is a perfect/passive construction: خورده (past participle 'eaten') + شد (auxiliary 'was/had been'), 'the bread was eaten'. کار می‌ساختند uses the imperfective/past habitual mi- form ('می‌ساختند') meaning 'they used to make / they set about making / they worked at'.
segments.s569-g52: سبک مایه is a collocation meaning 'light fare' or 'a small amount' (سبک 'light/small' + مایه 'substance/resource'). جایی here means 'somewhere' or 'at some place', and بپرداختند is the past plural 'they paid / they disbursed' (from پرداختن). Together: 'they paid/disbursed a small amount somewhere.'
segments.s569-g53: Word order: subject باغبان ('the gardener') with فعل داد 'gave' (past). سبک here describes the thing given ('a light/small [gift]'). می‌به is an archaic/poetic word of uncertain meaning in this line (appears as a noun between سبک and the recipient) and functions as the item given; شاپور is the indirect object/recipient, and داد is the past 'gave'.
segments.s569-g54: که introduces the content of what was said: بردار is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'take'. ازان = 'from that/of that', کس = 'person/anyone'. The final clause که آیدت یاد is a relative/temporal phrase 'who/which comes to your mind' — آیدت is 'comes to you' (آید + enclitic ت 'to you'), and یاد = 'memory/that which you remember.'
segments.s569-g55: بدو is the dative 'to him' (indicating the addressee); گفت is the past verb 'said' (here used as a verb: 'Shapur said to him ...'), with شاپور as the speaker. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the following noun میزبان 'host'.
segments.s569-g56: This phrase is a string of epithets modifying the addressee: سخن‌گوی used adjectivally 'eloquent', و 'and' links the adjectives, پرمایه 'abundant/substantial' modifies پالیزبان 'gardener/estate-keeper' — together forming the vocative description addressed to the host.

sentences.s569-s9: Night and day they rode on continuously, and they paid no attention to sleeping or to eating.
sentences.s569-s10: Thus from the city they pushed on toward Khuristan, riding as far as the land of Suristan.
sentences.s569-s11: When horse and body grew weak from the galloping, they sought a place to dismount.
sentences.s569-s12: A joyful village came before him on the road, full of gardens and fields and full of festive places.
sentences.s569-s5: A band of chosen, armed horsemen took two prized steeds from the rear.
sentences.s569-s6: There should also be a certain amount of dinars, and likewise (gifts) of fine drink, rubies, and every sort of thing.
sentences.s569-s7: When all the preparations for going were made, night came; the two persons firmly resolved on a plan.
sentences.s569-s8: They turned their faces toward the city of Iran; the two, joyful and keeping hidden, glad and seeking rest.
sentences.s569-s37: May our ill-wisher suffer as much harm as Caesar brought upon the Iranians.
sentences.s569-s38: Whoever he was, he was scattered from Iran; there remained in that land no cultivation (nor harvest).
sentences.s569-s39: Because of so much plunder and the killing of men and women, that great assembly was scattered.
sentences.s569-s40: And many of them likewise became pious; they girded themselves and stood before the cupbearer.
sentences.s569-s33: It was as though a crown sprang from your hair, and the hue of ivory from your face.
sentences.s569-s34: Shapur laughed and put the wine aside; a cold wind rose from his heart.
sentences.s569-s35: He said to the gardener, 'O Pak-din, what knowledge have you of the land of Iran?'
sentences.s569-s36: Thus he answered, 'O noble-minded one, may the ill-doer be far from you.'
sentences.s569-s41: Many a catholicos, with a hat upon his head, stood far from homeland and burial place.
sentences.s569-s42: Shapur the king said to him, "You made the face radiant like the moon of Ohrmazd."
sentences.s569-s43: How did it come about that Caesar grew so dominant? By fate, the lifeblood (water) of the Iranians turned dark.
sentences.s569-s44: The gardener said to him, 'O exalted one, may your preeminence be eternal, and may you have favor.'
sentences.s569-s49: The wise man recited this tale from the beginning — that whoever did not seek Azram as a guest.
sentences.s569-s50: No wisdom will be near him at all; his dark fate will bring need upon him.
sentences.s569-s51: Be at ease and rest, and eat to your content; when your heart becomes tamed, say your name.
sentences.s569-s52: Shapur said to him: 'That is fitting for the deaf one; now the king is our host.'
sentences.s569-s17: Shapur said to him, "O kind-hearted one, you ask a few words about the lost road,"
sentences.s569-s18: I am an Iranian man, a seeker of the road, fleeing, who has set his face toward this border.
sentences.s569-s19: I am full of my pain because of the Caesar and his host; may it not be that I see his head and helmet.
sentences.s569-s20: If tonight you give me lodging and keep watch and guard the border.
sentences.s569-s1: When the sun lifted its head from the tower of the Lion, the day brightened and banished sleep.
sentences.s569-s2: Those who had been in the town came to the feast; the great and the seekers came for the celebration.
sentences.s569-s3: The young maidservant turned her face toward a remedy, just as the people were seeking one.
sentences.s569-s4: When the portico was empty, it came into his grasp — his heart was seized: lionly courage, the harp, and the leopard came over him.
sentences.s569-s45: No trace of him, dead or alive, appeared for those proud men in Iran.
sentences.s569-s46: All those who had been from the native land are now wholly captives in Rome.
sentences.s569-s47: At this the gardener lamented bitterly — he who at that time had been the king’s host.
sentences.s569-s48: To him the steward said, ‘Stay here for three days; this house brightens the world.’
sentences.s569-s21: I mean that one day a tree will be useful to you — a tree that will bring a load to your ship.
sentences.s569-s22: The gardener said to him, 'This khan is yours; the gardener himself is also your guest.'
sentences.s569-s23: Concerning that cunning thing, I will try to get it within my reach; I will bring it and tell no one.
sentences.s569-s24: He dismounted from Shapur Shah's retinue/occasion, and the young maidservant went with him on the road.
sentences.s569-s29: Whoever brings it first, he ate it, for he was greater in years and in wisdom.
sentences.s569-s30: You are a little my senior in years; you ought, when you give, to eat.
sentences.s569-s31: To him the gardener said, 'O cultured one, the more beautiful should eat first.'
sentences.s569-s32: You should be foremost in front, for you are senior in breeding and in years.
sentences.s569-s13: His body, weary from suffering and fleeing evil, came and knocked at the gardener's door.
sentences.s569-s14: Then the gardener-man came near, who was both kind‑hearted and a good host.
sentences.s569-s15: Two men were seen, with spear and armor and themselves, and they asked Shapur, “Is this a greeting?”
sentences.s569-s16: On that very occasion he asked, ‘From where did you come, and did you prepare to charge thus?’
sentences.s569-s25: The gardener's wife made so many dishes; of every kind, as much as her power allowed.
sentences.s569-s26: When the bread was eaten, they set about their work; they paid out a small sum somewhere.
sentences.s569-s27: The gardener gave Shapur the small gift, saying, 'Take it from whichever person comes to your mind.'
sentences.s569-s28: Shapur said to him, 'O host, eloquent and richly endowed gardener.'
